manifest.description: Blueprint-compiled draft dispatch pack for math.found.predicate-logic (Phase 1 front-end; DRAFT only)

assets.title: Predicate Logic ‚Äî cognitive map
assets.content:
### Core Insight
Predicate logic extends propositional logic by quantifying over a domain: ‚àÄx P(x) claims P holds for every element, while ‚àÉx P(x) claims P holds for at least one. Quantifier order is critical ‚Äî swapping ‚àÄ and ‚àÉ changes the meaning, and often the truth value. Negation pushes through a quantifier by flipping ‚àÄ‚Üî‚àÉ and negating the predicate.

### Conceptual Hierarchy
```
Level 0 (Concrete): Predicates as sentence templates; domain as a specific set.
Level 1 (Pictorial): Truth table for small finite domains; scope diagram for nested quantifiers.
Level 2 (Abstract): Quantifier syntax (‚àÄx, ‚àÉx); negation laws; nested quantifiers.
Level 3 (Transfer): Analyse ‚àÄx ‚àÉy P(x,y) vs. ‚àÉy ‚àÄx P(x,y); different truth values, different meanings.
```

### Core Definitions
```
PREDICATE: P(x) is a sentence containing a variable x that becomes a proposition
  when x is assigned a value from the domain D.
  Example: P(x) = "x is even" over D = ‚Ñ§.
    P(4) is TRUE; P(7) is FALSE; P(x) alone has no truth value.

DOMAIN OF DISCOURSE (D): the set of values x can take.
  Example: D = ‚Ñï, D = ‚Ñ§, D = {1,2,...,10}.
  Changing D can change the truth value of ‚àÄx P(x) or ‚àÉx P(x).

UNIVERSAL QUANTIFIER ‚àÄ:
  ‚àÄx P(x) is TRUE iff P(d) is TRUE for EVERY d ‚àà D.
  ‚àÄx P(x) is FALSE iff P(d) is FALSE for SOME d ‚àà D (a counter-example suffices).

EXISTENTIAL QUANTIFIER ‚àÉ:
  ‚àÉx P(x) is TRUE iff P(d) is TRUE for AT LEAST ONE d ‚àà D.
  ‚àÉx P(x) is FALSE iff P(d) is FALSE for EVERY d ‚àà D.

NEGATION LAWS (De Morgan for quantifiers):
  ¬¨(‚àÄx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÉx ¬¨P(x)   [Not all ‚Üí exists one that fails]
  ¬¨(‚àÉx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÄx ¬¨P(x)   [None exists ‚Üí all fail]

NESTED QUANTIFIERS:
  ‚àÄx ‚àÉy P(x,y): For each x, we can FIND a (possibly different) y.
  ‚àÉy ‚àÄx P(x,y): There is ONE y that works for ALL x simultaneously.
  These are NOT equivalent in general.

FREE vs. BOUND VARIABLES:
  In ‚àÄx P(x,y): x is bound (quantified); y is free (unquantified).
  Replacing y with a value gives a proposition.
```

---

assets.title: [FOUNDATIONAL]: QUANTIFIER-SCOPE-COLLAPSE
assets.content:
### MC-1 [FOUNDATIONAL]: QUANTIFIER-SCOPE-COLLAPSE
**Belief:** ‚àÄx ‚àÉy P(x,y) and ‚àÉy ‚àÄx P(x,y) mean the same thing ‚Äî quantifier order doesn't matter.
**Origin:** Students read both as "for all x and for some y, P(x,y)" without registering the dependency. Multiplication is commutative; students assume logical quantifiers are too.
**Trigger:** "Is ‚àÄm ‚àÉn (m < n) the same as ‚àÉn ‚àÄm (m < n) over ‚Ñ§?" ‚Üí student says yes.
**Repair:** Over ‚Ñ§: ‚àÄm ‚àÉn (m<n) is TRUE (for any m, take n=m+1). But ‚àÉn ‚àÄm (m<n) is FALSE (no single integer exceeds all integers). Same predicate, reversed quantifiers, opposite truth values.
**Priority:** FOUNDATIONAL ‚Äî every proof involving "for all x, there exists y" depends on getting this right.

assets.title: NEGATION-FLIP-FAILURE
assets.content:
### MC-2: NEGATION-FLIP-FAILURE
**Belief:** ¬¨(‚àÄx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÄx ¬¨P(x) ‚Äî negation passes through the quantifier without flipping ‚àÄ‚Üî‚àÉ.
**Origin:** Students negate the predicate but forget to flip the quantifier, mirroring how ¬¨(p‚àßq) ‚â° ¬¨p‚àß¬¨q is incorrectly applied.
**Trigger:** "Negate: ‚àÄx (x > 0)" ‚Üí student writes "‚àÄx (x ‚â§ 0)" instead of "‚àÉx (x ‚â§ 0)."
**Repair:** The correct law is ¬¨(‚àÄx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÉx ¬¨P(x). Mnemonic: to disprove a "for all" claim, you need only ONE counter-example (there EXISTS one where it fails).

assets.title: DOMAIN-UNIVERSAL
assets.content:
### MC-3: DOMAIN-UNIVERSAL
**Belief:** The domain is always ‚Ñù (or "all numbers" or "everything") unless explicitly restricted; unstated domains default to ‚Ñù.
**Origin:** Most school examples use ‚Ñù or ‚Ñ§; students habituate to omitting domain specification.
**Trigger:** "‚àÄx (x¬≤ ‚â• 0)" ‚Äî student says TRUE without checking; over ‚ÑÇ it's FALSE (i¬≤ = ‚àí1 < 0).
**Repair:** Domain must always be stated. The same statement can be TRUE over D‚ÇÅ and FALSE over D‚ÇÇ.

---

assets.title: Concrete Foundation ‚Äî Predicates and Quantifiers on Finite Domains
assets.content: {"id":"TA-A01","title":"Concrete Foundation ‚Äî Predicates and Quantifiers on Finite Domains","primitives":["P03","P06","P49"],"addresses":["MC-3"]}

assets.title: Mastery Gate (P91 Terminal)
assets.content:
### TA-A04: Mastery Gate (P91 Terminal)

```
P77 ‚Äî MULTI-PROBLEM SET (4 problems)

Problem 1:
"Domain: D = {n ‚àà ‚Ñï : 1 ‚â§ n ‚â§ 10}. Predicate: S(n) = 'n is a perfect square.'
(a) List all n ‚àà D for which S(n) is TRUE.
(b) Determine the truth value of ‚àÄn S(n) and ‚àÉn S(n).
(c) Write the formal negation of ‚àÄn S(n) and state its truth value."

Problem 2:
"Write the formal negation of each statement (flip quantifier, negate predicate):
(a) ‚àÉx (x¬≤ = 2)  over ‚Ñö.
(b) ‚àÄx ‚àÄy (x + y = y + x)  over ‚Ñù."

Problem 3:
"Domain D = ‚Ñ§ √ó ‚Ñ§ (pairs of integers). Predicate P(a,b) = 'a + b = 0'.
(a) Determine the truth value of ‚àÄa ‚àÉb P(a,b).
(b) Determine the truth value of ‚àÉb ‚àÄa P(a,b).
(c) Explain in one sentence why (a) and (b) differ."

Problem 4:
"In the statement ‚àÄx ‚àÉy (y > x¬≤), identify:
(a) The bound variables.
(b) Any free variables.
(c) Whether this statement is TRUE or FALSE over domain ‚Ñù.
(d) Write its formal negation."
```

```
P55 ‚Äî SCORE (after P77)
Award 1 point per problem.
Running total before P76: __/4.
```

```
P76 ‚Äî TRANSFER PROBE (P76 independence: cross_links = [])
"Let D = ‚Ñï (positive integers 1, 2, 3, ‚Ä¶).
Define P(m, n) = 'm divides n' (meaning n = km for some k ‚àà ‚Ñï).

(a) State ‚àÄm ‚àÉn P(m,n) in plain English. Is it TRUE or FALSE over ‚Ñï?
    For each m, exhibit a witness n.

(b) State ‚àÉn ‚àÄm P(m,n) in plain English. Is it TRUE or FALSE over ‚Ñï?
    Explain why no such n can exist.

(c) Write the formal negation of statement (b). Simplify fully (no ¬¨ on a quantifier).

(d) In one sentence, explain what the difference between (a) and (b) reveals
    about why quantifier order matters in predicate logic."

[Expected:
(a) 'For every positive integer m, there exists a positive integer n that m divides.'
    TRUE: for any m, take n = m (m divides m, since m = 1¬∑m). Witness: n = m.
(b) 'There exists a positive integer n that is divisible by every positive integer.'
    FALSE: such an n would satisfy 1|n AND 2|n AND 3|n AND ‚Ä¶ ‚Äî no finite positive
    integer is divisible by all positive integers (e.g., n+1 doesn't divide n for n‚â•1; or
    directly: if n is divisible by every m, then n ‚â• m for all m, so n ‚â• n+1, contradiction).
(c) ¬¨(‚àÉn ‚àÄm P(m,n)) ‚â° ‚àÄn ‚àÉm ¬¨P(m,n) = ‚àÄn ‚àÉm (m does not divide n).
    In English: 'For every positive integer n, there is some positive integer m that does not divide n.'
(d) In (a), each m chooses its own n; in (b), a single n must work for every m ‚Äî
    this dependency is what makes (a) weaker than (b) and explains why swapping
    quantifiers changes truth values.]
```

```
P55 ‚Äî SCORE (after P76)
Award 1 point for P76.
Running total: __/5.
```

```
P75 ‚Äî MASTERY ASSESSMENT
Pass criterion: 4 out of 5 (‚åà0.80 √ó 5‚åâ = 4)
```

```
P55 ‚Äî SCORE (final)
Record pass/fail status.
```

```
P74 ‚Äî ROUTING DECISION
Score ‚â• 4/5 ‚Üí MASTERY ACHIEVED ‚Üí P78 COMPLETION
Score < 4/5 ‚Üí NEEDS REVIEW ‚Üí identify lowest-scoring item; activate Protocol B for its misconception
```

```
P55 ‚Äî SCORE (routing recorded)
```

```
P78 ‚Äî COMPLETION
"Predicate logic checkpoint complete. You have established:
  ‚Ä¢ Predicates P(x): sentences with a variable that become propositions when x is assigned a value.
  ‚Ä¢ Domain D: the set of values x can take; always state the domain.
  ‚Ä¢ ‚àÄx P(x): TRUE iff P(d) holds for every d ‚àà D; one counter-example makes it FALSE.
  ‚Ä¢ ‚àÉx P(x): TRUE iff P(d) holds for some d ‚àà D; one witness makes it TRUE.
  ‚Ä¢ Negation laws: ¬¨(‚àÄx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÉx ¬¨P(x); ¬¨(‚àÉx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÄx ¬¨P(x). Quantifier flips.
  ‚Ä¢ Nested quantifiers: ‚àÄx ‚àÉy ‚âÝ ‚àÉy ‚àÄx in general ‚Äî order determines dependency structure.
Next concepts: math.found.proof (quantifiers structure every mathematical proof),
math.found.set-theory (‚àÄ/‚àÉ over sets formalise membership and subset relations)."
```

---


## Component 5 ‚Äî Protocol B (Misconception Repair Chains)

### Repair Chain B-1: QUANTIFIER-SCOPE-COLLAPSE (MC-1 FOUNDATIONAL)

```
P64 ‚Äî CONCEPTUAL SHIFT
"BEFORE: ‚àÄx ‚àÉy P(x,y) and ‚àÉy ‚àÄx P(x,y) say the same thing.
 AFTER: They differ in DEPENDENCY. In ‚àÄx ‚àÉy, y can depend on x.
   In ‚àÉy ‚àÄx, y is fixed before x is chosen.

Dependency picture:
  ‚àÄx ‚àÉy P(x,y): Pick any x ‚Üí THEN search for y (y may vary with x).
  ‚àÉy ‚àÄx P(x,y): Fix y first ‚Üí THEN verify P holds for ALL x.

Concrete counter-example (‚Ñ§, P(m,n) = 'm < n'):
  ‚àÄm ‚àÉn (m<n): TRUE ‚Äî for any m, pick n=m+1.
  ‚àÉn ‚àÄm (m<n): FALSE ‚Äî no single integer beats all integers.
  Conclusion: swapping quantifiers changed TRUE to FALSE."

Repair exercise:
Over D = {1,2,3}, P(a,b) = 'a+b > 3'.
  ‚àÄa ‚àÉb P(a,b): a=1: b=3 ‚úì. a=2: b=2 ‚úì. a=3: b=1 ‚úì. TRUE.
  ‚àÉb ‚àÄa P(a,b): need fixed b with 1+b>3 AND 2+b>3 AND 3+b>3.
    b must satisfy b>2 AND b>1 AND b>0. So b>2. Only b=3.
    Check: 1+3=4>3 ‚úì, 2+3=5>3 ‚úì, 3+3=6>3 ‚úì. TRUE.
  (This example BOTH are true, showing independence isn't guaranteed either way.)
  Return to the ‚Ñ§ example for a pair that differs.

Exit gate: "Give one example where ‚àÄx ‚àÉy P(x,y) is TRUE but ‚àÉy ‚àÄx P(x,y) is FALSE."
Expected: ‚àÄm ‚àÉn (m<n) over ‚Ñ§ (TRUE) vs. ‚àÉn ‚àÄm (m<n) over ‚Ñ§ (FALSE).
‚Üí Correct: return to TA-A03.
‚Üí Incorrect: revisit the scope diagrams.
```

### Repair Chain B-2: NEGATION-FLIP-FAILURE (MC-2)

```
P41 ‚Äî MISCONCEPTION DETECTOR
Diagnostic: "Negate: ‚àÄx (x is a prime number) over D = {2,3,4,5}."
If student writes "‚àÄx (x is NOT a prime number)": MC-2 confirmed.

P27 ‚Äî MISCONCEPTION NAMING
"NEGATION-FLIP-FAILURE: ¬¨(‚àÄx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÄx ¬¨P(x) is WRONG.
The CORRECT law: ¬¨(‚àÄx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÉx ¬¨P(x).
Mnemonic: to disprove 'ALL x satisfy P,' show ONE x that DOESN'T satisfy P.
ONE counter-example = one existential statement."

P03 ‚Äî ANALOGY BRIDGE
"Prosecutor's claim: 'All suspects were at the scene.'
Defense's negation: 'Not ALL were at the scene' = 'AT LEAST ONE suspect was NOT.'
The defense does NOT say 'NONE were at the scene' ‚Äî that's ¬¨‚àÉ, not ¬¨‚àÄ."

Repair exercise:
Negate ‚àÉx (x¬≤ < 0) over ‚Ñù.
  ¬¨(‚àÉx (x¬≤<0)) ‚â° ‚àÄx ¬¨(x¬≤<0) ‚â° ‚àÄx (x¬≤ ‚â• 0). (TRUE over ‚Ñù.)
Negate ‚àÄx (x > 0) over ‚Ñ§.
  ¬¨(‚àÄx (x>0)) ‚â° ‚àÉx ¬¨(x>0) ‚â° ‚àÉx (x ‚â§ 0). (TRUE ‚Äî x=‚àí1 is a witness.)

Exit gate: "What are the two negation laws? State them symbolically."
Expected: ¬¨(‚àÄx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÉx ¬¨P(x) AND ¬¨(‚àÉx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÄx ¬¨P(x).
‚Üí Correct: return to TA-A02.
```

### Repair Chain B-3: DOMAIN-UNIVERSAL (MC-3)

```
P64 ‚Äî CONCEPTUAL SHIFT
"BEFORE: The domain is always ‚Ñù (or 'all numbers') by default.
 AFTER: The domain must always be specified; truth values can differ by domain.

Counter-example:
  ‚àÄx (x¬≤ ‚â• 0):
    Over ‚Ñù: TRUE (squares of real numbers are non-negative).
    Over ‚ÑÇ: FALSE ‚Äî take x=i; i¬≤=‚àí1 < 0.
  
  ‚àÄx (x + 1 > x):
    Over ‚Ñù: TRUE.
    Over ‚Ñ§/2‚Ñ§ = {[0],[1]}: [0]+[1]=[1] and [1]+[1]=[0].
    In [1]: [1]+[1]=[0] ‚â∞ [1]? Depends on ordering definition.

Lesson: ALWAYS state the domain. Same predicate, different domain ‚Üí possibly different truth value."

Exit gate: "Give an example of a predicate P(x) and two domains D‚ÇÅ, D‚ÇÇ such that
‚àÄx P(x) is TRUE over D‚ÇÅ but FALSE over D‚ÇÇ."
Expected: e.g. P(x) = 'x > 0'; D‚ÇÅ = ‚Ñï (TRUE), D‚ÇÇ = ‚Ñ§ (FALSE, x=‚àí1 is counter-example).
‚Üí Correct: return to TA-A01.
```

---


## Component 6 ‚Äî P89 Spaced Repetition Schedule

```
P89 ‚Äî SPACED REPETITION
Review schedule for math.found.predicate-logic:

Interval 1 (1 day after mastery):
  Recall prompt: "State the two negation laws for quantifiers.
  Negate: ‚àÉx (x is a factor of 7) over D = {2,3,4,5,6}."
  Target: ¬¨‚àÉ ‚â° ‚àÄ¬¨; negation: ‚àÄx (x is NOT a factor of 7); TRUE over D.

Interval 2 (3 days):
  Application: "Over D = ‚Ñù, compare:
  (a) ‚àÄx ‚àÉy (x¬∑y = 1)  and  (b) ‚àÉy ‚àÄx (x¬∑y = 1).
  Determine truth values and explain the dependency structure."
  Target: (a) FALSE ‚Äî x=0 has no multiplicative inverse;
          (b) FALSE ‚Äî y=1 fails for x=2 (2¬∑1‚âÝ1).

Interval 3 (7 days):
  Transfer: "Analyse ‚àÄŒµ>0 ‚àÉŒ¥>0 (|x‚àía|<Œ¥ ‚Üí |f(x)‚àíL|<Œµ).
  (a) What does this say about Œµ and Œ¥?
  (b) What does swapping the quantifiers change?"
  Target: (a) Œµ fixed first; then Œ¥ found (Œ¥ may depend on Œµ);
          (b) ‚àÉŒ¥ ‚àÄŒµ: one Œ¥ must work for ALL Œµ simultaneously ‚Äî much stronger,
              corresponds to uniform continuity, not pointwise.

Interval 4 (21 days):
  Integration: "When writing a proof that ‚àÄx ‚àà D, P(x), what is the
  standard proof strategy? How does predicate logic determine the proof structure?"
  Target: Let x ‚àà D be arbitrary; prove P(x). Generality of x represents ‚àÄ.
```

---


## Component 7 ‚Äî Cross-Blueprint Dependencies

### GR-8: Cross-link documentation
```
Incoming prerequisites:
  math.found.proposition ‚Üí Truth values (TRUE/FALSE); propositions as atomic statements.
    Predicates generalise propositions by introducing a free variable.
  math.found.logical-connectives ‚Üí ‚àß, ‚à®, ¬¨, ‚Üí, ‚Üî. The negation law
    ¬¨(‚àÄx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÉx ¬¨P(x) is the quantifier analogue of De Morgan's laws.

Outgoing unlocks:
  math.found.proof ‚Üí Every mathematical proof involves quantified statements;
    "‚àÄx P(x)" proofs use the arbitrary-element strategy; "‚àÉx P(x)" proofs require
    constructing a witness.
  math.found.set-theory ‚Üí Set membership (x ‚àà A) and subset (A ‚äÜ B, meaning
    ‚àÄx (x ‚àà A ‚Üí x ‚àà B)) are predicate logic statements.

Cross-links: none.
```

### GR-9: P76 Mode Determination
```
cross_links = []
P76 MODE: INDEPENDENCE
P76 probe: divisibility predicate P(m,n) = 'm divides n' over ‚Ñï;
  ‚àÄm ‚àÉn P(m,n) vs. ‚àÉn ‚àÄm P(m,n); negation of the existential form.
  Self-contained; no cross-concept dependency.
```

---


## Component 8 ‚Äî Teaching Notes

### Pedagogical Rationale
Predicate logic (bloom=analyze) requires students to break down quantified statements ‚Äî identify the quantifier, the domain, the predicate, and the dependency structure ‚Äî then evaluate truth values by checking elements or constructing counter-examples/witnesses. The bloom level means P49 branches and P77 problems require analysis, not just procedural symbol manipulation.

### Common Session Failure Modes
1. **MC-1 is the critical gate:** Students who conflate ‚àÄx ‚àÉy and ‚àÉy ‚àÄx will write flawed proofs indefinitely. The scope-dependency diagram (TA-A03, P11) is the most effective repair tool ‚Äî visualising "y is chosen AFTER x" vs. "y is fixed BEFORE x" separates the two cases.
2. **MC-2 persists even when corrected:** Students revert to ‚àÄx ¬¨P(x) under time pressure. The prosecutor/defense analogy in B-2 provides a durable mnemonic.
3. **Finite domain exercises are essential:** Abstract laws (‚àÄm ‚àÉn over ‚Ñ§) are easier to understand after smaller concrete checks (domain {1,2,3}). Always begin with finite domains in TA-A01 before moving to infinite ones.

### Connections to Broader Curriculum
- **Downstream ‚Äî math.found.proof:** Universal statements are proved by "let x ‚àà D be arbitrary"; existential statements by "let x = [specific witness]." The logic is directly predicate logic.
- **Downstream ‚Äî math.found.set-theory:** Every set operation (‚à©, ‚à™, ‚äÜ, complement) is a predicate-logic statement about membership. Set theory IS applied predicate logic.
- **Cross-domain ‚Äî calculus (later):** The Œµ-Œ¥ definition of limits (‚àÄŒµ>0 ‚àÉŒ¥>0 ‚Ä¶) is a nested quantifier statement. Students who master predicate logic here will find the limit definition much more legible.

### Language Precision
- Always say "for all x IN D" and "there exists x IN D" ‚Äî domain membership is essential.
- "Negate" means flip the quantifier AND negate the predicate; do not do one without the other.
- "Bound variable" vs "free variable": in ‚àÄx P(x,y), x is bound; y is free ‚Äî the statement is still a predicate in y, not a proposition.

---

### Automated Invariant Check (AIR)

- [x] AIR-2: No references to internal system variables.
- [x] AIR-3: cross_links = [] ‚Äî no concept IDs to validate.

assets.title: Scaffolding Protocol
assets.content:
### Entry Diagnostic (P41 gate)
```
P41 ‚Äî MISCONCEPTION DETECTOR
Prompt: "Consider P(x) = 'x¬≤ > x' and domain D = {‚àí2, ‚àí1, 0, 1, 2}.
(a) Evaluate P(x) for each element of D.
(b) Is ‚àÄx P(x) true or false?
(c) Is ‚àÉx P(x) true or false?"

CORRECT ((a): P(‚àí2)=4>‚àí2 ‚úì, P(‚àí1)=1>‚àí1 ‚úì, P(0)=0>0 ‚úó, P(1)=1>1 ‚úó, P(2)=4>2 ‚úì;
          (b) FALSE (P(0) and P(1) fail); (c) TRUE (P(2) holds)):
‚Üí No MC-1/MC-2 detected for this level ‚Üí proceed to TA-A01.

INCORRECT (part (b) TRUE ‚Äî misses the counter-examples):
‚Üí MC-1 potential. Ask follow-up: "Does P(0) hold? P(0) says 0¬≤ > 0, i.e., 0 > 0. True or false?"
‚Üí If student still says TRUE ‚Üí MC-1/MC-3 active ‚Üí activate Protocol B.

INCORRECT (part (c) FALSE ‚Äî misses all three TRUE cases):
‚Üí Ask: "Does P(2) hold? 4 > 2. True or false?"
‚Üí Guide to understanding ‚àÉ needs only one witness; activate B-2 if negation confusion surfaces.
```

### Prerequisite Bridge
Student completed math.found.proposition (truth values, logical structure) and math.found.logical-connectives (‚àß, ‚à®, ¬¨, ‚Üí, ‚Üî). Predicate logic extends this by adding a variable x and quantifiers ‚Äî ‚àÄ generalises "and for every element" and ‚àÉ generalises "or for some element."

### MAMR Enforcement
MC-1 is FOUNDATIONAL. If the student conflates quantifier order, correct this before domain specification or negation. MC-2 and MC-3 addressed FIFO once MC-1 cleared.

### CPA Progression: C ‚Üí P ‚Üí A
- **Concrete (C):** Small finite domain (e.g., {1,2,3,4,5}); evaluate predicate at each element; determine truth values of ‚àÄ and ‚àÉ by exhaustion.
- **Pictorial (P):** Scope diagram for nested quantifiers; dependency arrows (x selected first, y may depend on x).
- **Abstract (A):** Infinite domains (‚Ñ§, ‚Ñù); nested quantifiers; formal negation; free/bound variable identification.

---

assets.title: P89 Spaced Repetition Schedule
assets.content:
```
P89 ‚Äî SPACED REPETITION
Review schedule for math.found.predicate-logic:

Interval 1 (1 day after mastery):
  Recall prompt: "State the two negation laws for quantifiers.
  Negate: ‚àÉx (x is a factor of 7) over D = {2,3,4,5,6}."
  Target: ¬¨‚àÉ ‚â° ‚àÄ¬¨; negation: ‚àÄx (x is NOT a factor of 7); TRUE over D.

Interval 2 (3 days):
  Application: "Over D = ‚Ñù, compare:
  (a) ‚àÄx ‚àÉy (x¬∑y = 1)  and  (b) ‚àÉy ‚àÄx (x¬∑y = 1).
  Determine truth values and explain the dependency structure."
  Target: (a) FALSE ‚Äî x=0 has no multiplicative inverse;
          (b) FALSE ‚Äî y=1 fails for x=2 (2¬∑1‚âÝ1).

Interval 3 (7 days):
  Transfer: "Analyse ‚àÄŒµ>0 ‚àÉŒ¥>0 (|x‚àía|<Œ¥ ‚Üí |f(x)‚àíL|<Œµ).
  (a) What does this say about Œµ and Œ¥?
  (b) What does swapping the quantifiers change?"
  Target: (a) Œµ fixed first; then Œ¥ found (Œ¥ may depend on Œµ);
          (b) ‚àÉŒ¥ ‚àÄŒµ: one Œ¥ must work for ALL Œµ simultaneously ‚Äî much stronger,
              corresponds to uniform continuity, not pointwise.

Interval 4 (21 days):
  Integration: "When writing a proof that ‚àÄx ‚àà D, P(x), what is the
  standard proof strategy? How does predicate logic determine the proof structure?"
  Target: Let x ‚àà D be arbitrary; prove P(x). Generality of x represents ‚àÄ.
```

---

assets.title: Cross-Blueprint Dependencies
assets.content:
### GR-8: Cross-link documentation
```
Incoming prerequisites:
  math.found.proposition ‚Üí Truth values (TRUE/FALSE); propositions as atomic statements.
    Predicates generalise propositions by introducing a free variable.
  math.found.logical-connectives ‚Üí ‚àß, ‚à®, ¬¨, ‚Üí, ‚Üî. The negation law
    ¬¨(‚àÄx P(x)) ‚â° ‚àÉx ¬¨P(x) is the quantifier analogue of De Morgan's laws.

Outgoing unlocks:
  math.found.proof ‚Üí Every mathematical proof involves quantified statements;
    "‚àÄx P(x)" proofs use the arbitrary-element strategy; "‚àÉx P(x)" proofs require
    constructing a witness.
  math.found.set-theory ‚Üí Set membership (x ‚àà A) and subset (A ‚äÜ B, meaning
    ‚àÄx (x ‚àà A ‚Üí x ‚àà B)) are predicate logic statements.

Cross-links: none.
```

```
cross_links = []

  Self-contained; no cross-concept dependency.
```
